answer:
Dunoyer's ending is decisively more programmatic. Comparing the two:

**Comte, Traité de Législation Vol II, Chapter 27** (work_slug: charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d) is a purely descriptive ethnographic chapter surveying the social organization and moral conditions of African peoples (Congo, western coast between the tropics vs. the Cape). Its final paragraphs draw comparative observations — that fertile land produces greater subjugation, that agricultural peoples face slavery rather than extermination, that harsh subordination is a lesser evil than constant savage warfare — but offer no prescriptive agenda, no reform call, no forward-looking program. It ends as it begins: as careful cross-cultural analysis with no directive content.

**Dunoyer, Nouveau traité d'économie Vol I, Chapter 12** (work_slug: charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce) ends with a formal 'Conclusion:' section laying out four explicit propositions about social states: (1) perfect equality is impossible; (2) the current coexistence of growing opulence and growing misery is real; (3) a state where misery is contained and well-being spreads is 'realizable, but not yet realized: it is the state toward which we must tend'; (4) a state without any miserable people is distant but not contradictory with nature. This is a manifesto structure — a graded program pointing society toward a specific realizable goal.

**Dunoyer, Nouveau traité d'économie Vol II, Chapter 19** (work_slug: charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03) is even more expansive in its prescriptive content: it calls for the wholesale reform of intellectual education (abandon classical languages, orient schools toward the arts men actually practice), demands the abolition of government monopolies over printing and teaching, and closes with quantitative evidence of intellectual progress as an argument for removing all remaining obstacles. The final sentence — 'there will be no progress that does not serve; a people will not have perfected a single one of the means of labor in its application to the culture of intelligences without one seeing this culture act with more power' — is a forward-looking principle implying an ongoing agenda of liberalization.

In short: Comte's chapter 27 closes as ethnographic inventory; both Dunoyer closing chapters close as reform programs. Dunoyer Vol I is the more formally programmatic (explicit four-point conclusion with a prescribed direction); Dunoyer Vol II is the more substantively programmatic (a comprehensive agenda for intellectual liberty and educational reform). Either way, Dunoyer is far more programmatic than Comte.

tool_calls.name: list_works
tool_calls.result_summary: Found 6 works by Charles Comte including Traité de Législation Vols I-IV and Traité de la propriété Vols I-II

tool_calls.name: list_works
tool_calls.result_summary: Found 2 works by Charles Dunoyer: Nouveau traité d'économie Vol I (chapters 0-12) and Vol II (chapters 13-19)

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Final chapter of Comte Vol II: descriptive ethnography of west African peoples' feudal organization and morals. No programmatic content; ends with comparative observations about the relative well-being of agricultural vs. savage peoples.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Final chapter of Dunoyer Vol I: analysis of inevitable inequalities in the industrial regime. Ends with formal four-point 'Conclusion' including the prescriptive statement 'it is the state toward which we must tend' regarding containment of misery.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Final chapter of Dunoyer Vol II: comprehensive chapter on freedom of intellectual arts. Strongly programmatic throughout — calls for educational reform, abolition of government monopolies over printing and teaching. Ends with statistics on intellectual progress as argument for further liberalization.
